one_line_takeaway: Expensive Feldman-style memorization scores can be swapped for cheap proxies (confidence, binary accuracy, holdout retraining) inside the RUM unlearning meta-algorithm, preserving accuracy and MIA-privacy gains while cutting score-computation cost by up to ~99.98%.
key_finding: Cheap memorization proxies deliver essentially the same unlearning gains as exact memorization at a tiny fraction of the cost. Confidence and binary accuracy correlate strongly (negatively) with memorization (Spearman -0.80 and -0.71 on CIFAR-10; -0.91 and -0.89 on CIFAR-100), and all three selected proxies improve both accuracy (ToW) and privacy (ToW-MIA) when integrated into RUMF, sometimes surpassing memorization itself. The authors report up to a 30% accuracy improvement and up to a 46% privacy improvement over baselines. Holdout retraining is the standout for practicality: it needs no intervention during training and costs up to 99.98% less runtime than computing exact memorization scores, while on CIFAR-10 (no data augmentation) it even beats other proxies and memorization. The stability analysis shows RUMF's edge over vanilla shrinks as successive unlearning steps strip out the most-memorized long-tail examples, evidenced by a falling Gini coefficient (e.g., CIFAR-10 RUMF 0.788 at step 0 -> 0.642 at step 5; Tiny-ImageNet RUMF 0.629 -> 0.365).
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is an empirical study showing that expensive-to-compute exact memorization scores (Feldman-style) can be replaced by cheap memorization-score PROXIES inside memorization-based machine unlearning without sacrificing unlearning quality. Note the scope carefully: the unlearning meta-algorithm RUM/RUMF is prior work by Zhao et al. [23]; the memorization score is Feldman [8]; and every proxy studied (C-score [14], learning-event proxies confidence/max-confidence/entropy/binary-accuracy [14,20], holdout retraining [5], loss curvature [11]) is also prior work. What this paper contributes is: (1) a fidelity-vs-efficiency profiling of these proxies against true memorization; (2) selecting the three best (confidence, binary accuracy, holdout retraining) and plugging them into RUMF in place of memorization scores; (3) demonstrating that proxies match or even beat exact memorization on both an accuracy metric (ToW) and a privacy metric while cutting proxy-computation cost by up to ~99.98%; (4) introducing ToW-MIA, a membership-inference variant of the ToW metric; and (5) a stability analysis of sequential/iterative unlearning using Gini coefficients and Lorenz curves to explain when the RUMF advantage erodes.
threat_model: This is a privacy-motivated paper rather than an adversarial-defense paper, and the review does not invent an attacker. The motivating privacy concern is the need to remove the influence of privacy-sensitive, poisoned, or otherwise undesirable training examples from an already-trained model (right-to-be-forgotten / GDPR framing, refs [13,19]). The only concrete adversary in the paper is the membership-inference attacker used purely as an EVALUATION probe: a binary classifier trained to distinguish retain-set (Dr) from test-set (Dt) points is queried on forget-set (Df) points, and effective unlearning is signaled when forgotten examples are classified as 'non-training.' The MIA-gap (difference from retrain-from-scratch MIA) feeds the ToW-MIA metric. There is no claim of robustness against an adaptive or worst-case adversary; MIA is a yardstick, not a defended-against threat.
limitations: Loss curvature, despite decent fidelity (Spearman 0.69-0.70), is impractically slow (197%-243% of full retraining time) and is dropped. No single proxy wins across all datasets/algorithms, especially on the privacy (ToW-MIA) side. Holdout retraining's advantage is tied to the absence of data augmentation and shrinks once augmentation is used (CIFAR-100, Tiny-ImageNet). Scope is narrow: only image classification, three modest datasets, small CNN backbones (ResNet-18/50, VGG-16), a single meta-algorithm variant (RUMF, refinement-only) and one MIA construction; forget-set size is fixed at 3,000. The stability finding is partly an artifact of the experimental design (unlearning an unusually large fraction of data); the authors themselves note that in realistic settings only a tiny fraction is unlearned, leaving the memorization distribution roughly unchanged. The paper is a NeurIPS 2024 fine-tuning workshop paper, so breadth is limited.

quantitative_results: Proxy fidelity (Spearman corr. with memorization), CIFAR-10 / CIFAR-100: confidence -0.80 / -0.91; max confidence -0.76 / -0.87; entropy -0.75 / -0.80; binary accuracy -0.71 / -0.89; holdout retraining 0.67 / 0.62; loss curvature 0.69 / 0.70

Holdout retraining compute cost is 0.017% (CIFAR-10) and 0.001% (CIFAR-100) of exact memorization computation, i.e. up to 99.98%+ cheaper; loss curvature by contrast costs 197.3% / 243.3% of full retraining time
Headline gains from integrating proxies into RUMF: up to 30% accuracy (ToW) improvement and up to 46% privacy (ToW-MIA) improvement over baselines
CIFAR-100/ResNet-50 NegGrad+ with holdout-retraining proxy: RUMF ToW 0.966 +/- 0.042 vs vanilla 0.879 +/- 0.046; ToW-MIA RUMF 0.928 vs vanilla 0.790
CIFAR-10/ResNet-18 SalUn with holdout-retraining proxy: RUMF ToW 0.956 vs vanilla 0.858; ToW-MIA RUMF 0.939 vs vanilla 0.643

Gini coefficient of proxy values over 5 sequential NegGrad+ steps: CIFAR-10 RUMF 0.788->0.642 (vanilla 0.788->0.723); Tiny-ImageNet RUMF 0.629->0.365 (vanilla 0.629->0.403), showing skew reduction that erodes the RUMF advantage
RUMF runtime is roughly 2x the vanilla approach but far below retrain-from-scratch (e.g. CIFAR-100 retrain ~6,127-6,430s vs RUMF unlearning runtimes ~770-860s)
Experimental config: forget set Df = 3,000 examples, K=3 subsets of 1,000 (lowest/medium/highest proxy), results averaged over 3 runs with 95% CIs
baselines_compared: Approximate unlearning algorithms: Fine-tune [22,12], NegGrad+ [15], L1-sparse [16], SalUn [7]
RUM application modes: vanilla (one-shot) and shuffle (random partition) vs RUMF (proxy-refined partition)
Reference signal: exact Feldman memorization scores [8,9] vs the proxies
Proxies evaluated: confidence, max confidence, entropy, binary accuracy (learning-event proxies [14,20]), holdout retraining [5], loss curvature [11]
